expectations.text: The deadline or timeline change appears in the first line or two of the rewritten message
expectations.evidence: The BLUF version opens with: 'Heads up: the migration project deadline has moved to March 28.' — the deadline is the very first sentence.

expectations.text: The backstory (infra delays, scope changes, alignment issues) does not appear in the opening
expectations.evidence: The opening line contains only the deadline. Backstory appears in the second paragraph: 'A few things contributed to the shift — infra delays, scope changes, and stakeholder alignment.' — clearly secondary and subordinate.

expectations.text: The rewritten message is meaningfully shorter than the original
expectations.evidence: Original message is approximately 65 words across six sentences. The BLUF rewrite is approximately 35 words across two short sentences — roughly 46% shorter.

expectations.text: The feedback identifies that the key information (deadline) was buried at the end
expectations.evidence: The 'What changed and why' section states explicitly: 'In the original, "The new deadline is March 28" is buried near the end after six sentences of context.'

suggestions: Consider adding a word count comparison in the output to make the 'meaningfully shorter' assertion more immediately verifiable.
The feedback section could explicitly label the buried-deadline problem as a BLUF violation to reinforce the skill's core concept.
eval_feedback.overall: Strong output. All four assertions pass clearly. The BLUF rewrite leads with the deadline, demotes context to a secondary line, and the feedback accurately diagnoses why the original was ineffective. The explanation in 'What changed and why' is detailed and pedagogically sound.
